hero.title: Python Interview Questions
hero.description: From basic syntax to advanced concurrency and data science concepts. Use the practice mode to hide answers and test your knowledge.

beginner.q: What are the key features of Python?
beginner.a: Python is an interpreted, high-level, dynamically typed, and garbage-collected language. It supports multiple programming paradigms (OOP, procedural, functional) and has a massive standard library.

beginner.q: What is the difference between a list and a tuple?
beginner.a: Lists are mutable (can be changed after creation) and use square brackets `[]`. Tuples are immutable (cannot be changed) and use parentheses `()`. Tuples are generally faster and safer for fixed data.

beginner.q: What is a dictionary in Python?
beginner.a: A dictionary is an unordered collection of key-value pairs. It is mutable, keys must be unique and immutable (like strings or numbers), and it is highly optimized for retrieving values.

intermediate.a: Decorators are functions that take another function as an argument and extend its behavior without explicitly modifying it. They are denoted by the `@decorator_name` syntax.
`*args` allows a function to accept any number of positional arguments (passed as a tuple). `**kwargs` allows a function to accept any number of keyword arguments (passed as a dictionary).

intermediate.q: What is the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL)?
intermediate.a: The GIL is a mutex that protects access to Python objects, preventing multiple threads from executing Python bytecodes at once. This makes pure Python threads unsuitable for CPU-bound tasks, though they work well for I/O-bound tasks.

advanced.q: How does Python handle memory management?
advanced.a: Python uses reference counting for memory management. When an object's reference count drops to zero, it is deallocated. It also has a cyclic garbage collector to detect and clean up reference cycles.
A metaclass is a class whose instances are classes. Just as an ordinary class defines the behavior of certain objects, a metaclass defines the behavior of certain classes and their instances.
Generators are functions that return an iterable sequence of items one by one rather than all at once, saving memory. They use the `yield` keyword instead of `return` to pause and resume their state.
